system_name: Allegro PoC — Java Swing MVP + WebSocket Client
architectural_style: Desktop client application with MVP pattern

layers.name: Presentation Layer
layers.description: Swing UI components and Presenter logic handling all user interaction.

layers.name: Model Layer
layers.description: Domain model, business logic orchestration, event management, and data structures.

layers.name: Service / Integration Layer
layers.description: External system integration — HTTP POST to backend API.

layers.name: WebSocket Client Layer
layers.description: Standalone WebSocket client application with embedded Swing UI (not integrated with MVP module).

components.name: PocView
responsibilities: Provide Swing component references to Presenter

external_dependencies.name: HTTPBin Docker Container
external_dependencies.purpose: Echo back form data as JSON response for PoC demonstration

external_dependencies.name: Node.js WebSocket Server
external_dependencies.purpose: Broadcast messages to connected Swing WebSocket clients

design_patterns.description: Passive View variant where PocPresenter drives all interaction logic. PocView holds no logic.
Custom synchronous pub-sub implementation for decoupling model outcome notification from presenter.
Type-safe wrapper used as a data cell for all form fields.
Using an Enum as map key ensures compile-time safety for field names.
javax.websocket annotation-driven client endpoint with lifecycle callbacks.

architectural_decisions.rationale: MVP provides better testability for Swing applications as the Presenter can be tested without the actual View. The Passive View variant used here maximises this benefit.
A custom EventEmitter provides a simpler, string-based event interface rather than Java's built-in bean event system, more similar to JavaScript EventEmitter style.
For PoC purposes, HTTPBin echos back any JSON sent to it, allowing form submission round-trip to be demonstrated without a real backend.
Tyrus provides a complete JSR-356 WebSocket client implementation that can run in a standalone Java SE application without a full application server.
The websocket.Main is a separate, earlier prototype that was not refactored to use MVP. It represents a different development timeline than the com.poc module.
